description: Keyed tabular encoding - objects whose values are uniform objects collapse into keyed headers with entry rows

tests.name: encodes objects of uniform objects in keyed tabular form
encodes an eligible root object in keyless keyed form
tests.note: The keyless keyed form is valid only at the root (§5)

tests.name: collapses uniform nested object columns inside keyed headers
orders fields by the first entry value's encounter order
uses the active delimiter in keyed headers and entry-row cells
tests.note: The keyed marker precedes the delimiter symbol: [2:|]

tests.name: quotes entry keys per key encoding
tests.note: Entry keys follow §7.3, the same rule as object keys

tests.name: quotes entry-row cells containing the active delimiter
keeps single-entry objects in nested form
tests.note: Keyed tabular detection requires at least two entries

tests.name: keeps objects in nested form when entry values have differing key sets
tests.expected:
envs:
  dev:
    host: x
  prod:
    host: y
    port: 1

tests.name: keeps objects in nested form when a value is primitive
keeps objects in nested form when an entry value contains an array
tests.expected:
m:
  a:
    tags[1]: x
  b:
    tags[1]: y
tests.note: Entry-row cells must be primitive leaves; an array value disqualifies the column

tests.name: emits a keyed header on the hyphen line when it is the first field of a list item
tests.expected:
items[2]:
  - config[2:]{x}:
      a: 1
      b: 2
    status: ok
  - status: down
tests.note: Entry rows at depth +2, sibling fields at depth +1, mirroring tabular arrays on hyphen lines

tests.name: never encodes an anonymous array element in keyed form
tests.note: The element object is keyed-eligible but has no key; the keyless form is root-only, so it encodes per §10
